subtopic_detail: Day 4 neonatal jaundice approaching exchange threshold
stem: A term male infant on day 4 of life is brought to the special care nursery with progressive jaundice. He was born at 39 weeks by spontaneous vaginal delivery with a birth weight of 3.4 kg, now 3.1 kg. Mother is blood group O Rh positive and the infant is A Rh positive with a positive direct antiglobulin test. He is breastfeeding only with reduced wet nappies. The serum bilirubin has risen by 11 micromol/L per hour over the past 6 hours and is now 460 micromol/L; the exchange threshold for this infant is 480 micromol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168, RR 56, T 36.8, SpO2 97% room air
data_table.Examination: Cephalad jaundice extending to soles, sleepy but rousable, mild dehydration, no neurological abnormality
data_table.Investigations: Total SBR 460 micromol/L (exchange threshold 480), conjugated 18 micromol/L, Hb 152 g/L, DAT positive, reticulocytes 8%, weight loss 9% from birth

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Continue single phototherapy with breastfeeding support and repeat bilirubin in 6 hours
options.rationale: Single phototherapy is inadequate when bilirubin is within 30 micromol/L of the exchange threshold and rising rapidly with positive DAT. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

options.text: Immediate single volume exchange transfusion without trial of intensive phototherapy first
options.rationale: Intensive phototherapy with IVIG often averts the need for exchange when bilirubin is within range of the exchange threshold and not yet exceeded. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

options.text: Cease breastfeeding for 24 hours and switch to formula to exclude breast milk jaundice diagnosis
options.rationale: Cessation of breastfeeding is not the management of an acutely rising haemolytic jaundice at threshold. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

options.text: Intensive phototherapy on three sides with IV immunoglobulin 1 g/kg and prepare for exchange
options.rationale: Rising bilirubin near the exchange threshold with haemolytic disease is managed with intensive phototherapy, IVIG to reduce haemolysis, and exchange transfusion preparation. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

options.text: Phenobarbital 5 mg/kg twice daily to induce hepatic glucuronidation as bridging therapy
options.rationale: Phenobarbital is not standard acute therapy for haemolytic neonatal jaundice in current Australian neonatal practice. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

explanation.summary: Neonatal jaundice approaching the exchange transfusion threshold in haemolytic disease is a neonatal emergency. Australian guidance recommends intensive phototherapy on multiple sides, intravenous immunoglobulin (1 g/kg) for confirmed haemolytic disease when bilirubin remains within 30 to 50 micromol/L of the exchange threshold or rises rapidly, adequate hydration including possible intravenous fluids, and exchange transfusion preparation. Exchange transfusion replaces the infant blood volume twice, removing bilirubin, antibody, and sensitised red cells. Long-term sequelae of acute bilirubin encephalopathy include athetoid cerebral palsy, hearing loss, and gaze abnormalities.
key_points: Intensive phototherapy, IVIG and exchange preparation are the response to bilirubin near threshold in haemolytic disease
DAT positive ABO or Rh incompatibility increases haemolytic load and the rate of bilirubin rise
Exchange transfusion thresholds depend on gestation, postnatal age, and risk factors
explanation.pearls: Intravenous immunoglobulin 1 g/kg reduces the need for exchange transfusion in haemolytic disease of the newborn when bilirubin is within 30 to 50 micromol/L of the exchange threshold.

sources.label: NICE NG98: Jaundice in newborn babies under 28 days

subtopic_detail: Complicated pneumonia with parapneumonic effusion management
stem: A 6-year-old boy was admitted 3 days ago with right lower lobe community acquired pneumonia and started on intravenous benzylpenicillin 50 mg/kg six hourly. He has had persistent fevers to 39 degrees Celsius, increasing right-sided chest pain, and rising oxygen requirement. Repeat chest radiograph shows a moderate right pleural effusion. Bedside ultrasound shows 4 cm depth of fluid with internal septations.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 142, RR 38, T 39.1, SpO2 92% on 2 L/min via nasal prongs
data_table.Examination: Reduced air entry and stony dull percussion right base, splinting on inspiration, no shift of trachea
data_table.Investigations: CRP 220 mg/L, WCC 22 x10^9/L; ultrasound right effusion 4 cm depth with internal septations; blood cultures pending

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Diagnostic pleural tap only with ongoing intravenous benzylpenicillin and no indwelling drain
options.rationale: Simple diagnostic tap is inadequate to drain a 4 cm septated empyema and leaves the infection undrained. (Source: RCH CPG: Pneumonia)

options.text: Continue benzylpenicillin monotherapy and reassess clinical state in 48 hours without drainage
options.rationale: Persistent fever and rising oxygen requirement with a septated effusion are indications for drainage and broader cover, not continued observation. (Source: RCH CPG: Pneumonia)

options.text: Refer to cardiothoracic surgery for primary video-assisted thoracoscopic decortication today
options.rationale: VATS is reserved for failure of chest drain with intrapleural fibrinolytic in Australian paediatric practice, not first-line for an unoperated septated effusion. (Source: RCH CPG: Pneumonia)

options.text: Switch to broader spectrum antibiotics with intrapleural fibrinolytic delivered chest drain
options.rationale: Septated paediatric parapneumonic effusion failing antibiotics is treated with broader cover plus image-guided chest drain and intrapleural fibrinolytic per AU practice. (Source: RCH CPG: Pneumonia)

options.text: Oral switch to amoxicillin clavulanate 25 mg/kg three times daily and discharge home today
options.rationale: Oral switch is inappropriate in a child with rising oxygen requirement, persistent sepsis features, and an undrained septated effusion. (Source: RCH CPG: Pneumonia)

explanation.summary: Paediatric parapneumonic effusion progresses through exudative, fibrinopurulent, and organising stages. Empyema (frank pus, positive culture, or pH less than 7.2) and septated effusion failing initial antibiotics are managed by broader spectrum intravenous antibiotics covering Streptococcus pneumoniae, Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pyogenes plus image-guided chest drainage with intrapleural fibrinolytic. Most children avoid surgical decortication; video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery is reserved for failure of medical and drain-based therapy. Antibiotics typically continue for at least 2 to 4 weeks total with oral step-down once clinically improving.
key_points: Septated effusion failing antibiotics requires drainage and broader spectrum cover
Intrapleural fibrinolytic via chest drain is the AU paediatric step before VATS
Total antibiotic duration is at least 2 to 4 weeks with oral step-down after clinical improvement
explanation.pearls: Intrapleural urokinase via chest drain twice daily for 3 days clears most paediatric septated parapneumonic effusions without recourse to VATS decortication.

sources.label: BTS Guideline: pleural infection in children

subtopic_detail: Heavy menstrual bleeding: first-line management in a 38 year old
stem: A 38-year-old woman presents with a 12 month history of progressively heavier and prolonged menses, with passage of clots and tiredness. She has two children and her family is complete. Pelvic ultrasound shows a normal sized uterus with no fibroids or polyps. Her cervical screening is up to date. Her ferritin is 14 micrograms/L and Hb is 109 g/L. She is otherwise well, with regular cycles every 28 days, and is not pursuing pregnancy.

data_table.Examination: Pelvic examination normal, no adnexal mass; speculum normal cervix
data_table.Investigations: Hb 109 g/L, ferritin 14 micrograms/L, TSH 2.1 mIU/L, beta-hCG negative, TVUS normal uterus and ovaries

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.text: Total laparoscopic hysterectomy after iron infusion as a definitive primary management plan
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is a definitive option after medical therapy has been tried, not first-line for a woman who has not yet tried the LNG-IUS. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding)

options.text: Oral tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily on day 1 to 4 of menses as long-term therapy only
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid is effective but second to the 52 mg LNG-IUS as first-line in suitable women and does not provide contraception. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding)

options.text: Endometrial ablation now as definitive treatment with no other contraceptive considerations
options.rationale: Endometrial ablation is reserved for women in whom medical therapy has failed and who do not desire fertility, and requires effective contraception. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding)

options.text: Insertion of a 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system at the next available appointment
options.rationale: AU and international guidance recommends the 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system as the first-line medical therapy for ovulatory heavy menstrual bleeding without structural cause. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding)

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill cyclically with iron supplementation and review in 6 months
options.rationale: Combined oral contraceptives reduce menstrual loss less effectively than the LNG-IUS and have contraindications that limit use. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding)

explanation.summary: Heavy menstrual bleeding without structural pathology in a woman who has completed her family is managed first with the 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system, which reduces blood loss by around 90% and provides effective contraception. Alternatives include tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily on bleeding days, NSAIDs, combined hormonal contraceptives, and progestogen regimens; each suits specific clinical settings. Surgical options (endometrial ablation, hysterectomy) follow failure of medical therapy. Investigation of heavy menstrual bleeding includes full blood count, ferritin, TSH, coagulation if indicated, and pelvic ultrasound to exclude structural causes.
key_points: 52 mg LNG-IUS is first-line medical therapy for ovulatory heavy menstrual bleeding without structural cause
Tranexamic acid is a useful non-hormonal short-course option but less effective than LNG-IUS
Surgical options follow failure of medical therapy
explanation.pearls: The 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system reduces menstrual blood loss by approximately 90% over 12 months and remains effective for 8 years for contraception and 5 to 7 years for heavy menstrual bleeding.

subtopic_detail: Vulvovaginal candidiasis vs bacterial vaginosis vs trichomoniasis
stem: A 26-year-old non-pregnant woman presents with a 5 day history of vulval itch, soreness, and a thick white discharge. She has had two similar episodes in the last 12 months treated with over-the-counter therapy. She is in a monogamous heterosexual relationship and uses combined oral contraceptive pill. There is no abnormal bleeding or pelvic pain. Speculum examination shows a thick white adherent discharge and erythema; pH of vaginal fluid is 4.2 and there is no fishy odour.

data_table.Examination: Vulval erythema, thick adherent curdy white discharge, no cervical motion tenderness, no adnexal mass, vaginal pH 4.2, negative amine test
data_table.Investigations: Microscopy pseudohyphae visible; high vaginal swab for STIs sent; urine pregnancy test negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for 7 days for bacterial vaginosis treatment cover
options.rationale: Bacterial vaginosis presents with thin grey discharge, fishy odour, and pH above 4.5, not curdy discharge with pH 4.2. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: bacterial vaginosis)

options.text: Single dose oral fluconazole 150 mg with patient education on triggers and self-care
options.rationale: Uncomplicated vulvovaginal candidiasis in a non-pregnant woman is treated with a single oral 150 mg fluconazole dose or a short topical imidazole course. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: vulvovaginal candidiasis)

options.text: Oral tinidazole 2 g single dose for both partners as empirical trichomoniasis treatment
options.rationale: Trichomoniasis gives a frothy yellow-green discharge with strawberry cervix and high pH, not the picture here. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: trichomoniasis)

options.text: Topical hydrocortisone 1% twice daily for 2 weeks without antifungal therapy added today
options.rationale: Topical steroids alone do not treat candidiasis and may worsen if used without antifungal cover. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: vulvovaginal candidiasis)

options.text: Oral cefalexin 500 mg four times daily for 7 days based on empirical bacterial superinfection
options.rationale: Oral cephalosporins do not treat vulvovaginal candidiasis and may worsen by altering bacterial flora. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: vulvovaginal candidiasis)

explanation.summary: Differentiation of common vaginal discharge causes is by history, vaginal pH and microscopy. Vulvovaginal candidiasis gives curdy white discharge with pH under 4.5, vulval itch and burning, and yeasts and pseudohyphae on microscopy; treatment is oral fluconazole 150 mg single dose or topical imidazole for 1 to 7 days. Bacterial vaginosis is a thin grey discharge with fishy odour, pH above 4.5, and clue cells, treated with metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for 7 days or topical clindamycin. Trichomoniasis is a sexually transmitted infection causing frothy yellow-green discharge with strawberry cervix and high pH, treated with tinidazole 2 g or metronidazole 2 g single dose plus partner treatment.
key_points: Vaginal pH and discharge character separate candidiasis, BV, and trichomoniasis
Oral fluconazole 150 mg single dose treats uncomplicated candidiasis
Recurrent candidiasis (4 or more episodes per year) prompts longer induction maintenance and trigger review
explanation.pearls: Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis is defined as 4 or more episodes in 12 months and is treated with induction oral fluconazole 150 mg every 72 hours for 3 doses then weekly for 6 months.

subtopic_detail: Contraceptive choice for a woman on enzyme-inducing antiepileptic
stem: A 24-year-old woman with epilepsy currently controlled on carbamazepine 400 mg twice daily presents to discuss contraception. She is sexually active with her male partner and uses condoms inconsistently. She would prefer not to take a daily pill and has no contraindications to hormonal contraception. Her cycles are regular and her last menstrual period was 1 week ago. She is not breastfeeding.

data_table.Investigations: Beta hCG negative; no current sexually transmitted infection screen abnormalities

lead_in: What is the most appropriate contraceptive option?

options.text: Standard depot medroxyprogesterone acetate 150 mg intramuscular every 12 weeks consistently
options.rationale: Depot medroxyprogesterone has the disadvantage of bone density loss with long-term use and offers no advantage over the copper IUD in this patient. (Source: RANZCOG: Long-acting reversible contraception)

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill with 50 micrograms ethinylestradiol as enhanced dose therapy
options.rationale: Enzyme inducers reduce combined hormonal contraceptive efficacy unpredictably; higher dose ethinylestradiol is not recommended in current AU practice. (Source: RANZCOG: Long-acting reversible contraception)

options.text: Etonogestrel subdermal implant on the same day with no contraceptive interaction concern
options.rationale: Etonogestrel implant is reduced in efficacy by enzyme inducers like carbamazepine and is not recommended. (Source: RANZCOG: Long-acting reversible contraception)

options.text: Progestogen-only pill at standard daily dose with backup condoms during enzyme inducer use
options.rationale: Enzyme inducers also reduce progestogen-only pill efficacy and dose-doubling is not licensed. (Source: RANZCOG: Long-acting reversible contraception)

options.text: Copper intrauterine device inserted at the next available appointment for long-term cover
options.rationale: Non-hormonal copper IUD is not affected by enzyme-inducing antiepileptic medication and is recommended as a first-line long-acting contraceptive in this setting. (Source: RANZCOG: Long-acting reversible contraception)

explanation.summary: Enzyme-inducing antiepileptics (carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital, primidone, topiramate above 200 mg daily, oxcarbazepine, rufinamide) accelerate metabolism of oestrogens and progestogens and reduce the efficacy of combined hormonal methods, progestogen-only pills, and the etonogestrel implant. AU recommendations favour the copper or 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine systems because intrauterine levels are unaffected. Depot medroxyprogesterone is acceptable but carries other long-term considerations. Valproate, lamotrigine alone, and most newer anticonvulsants do not significantly induce contraceptive metabolism but lamotrigine levels fall with combined hormonal contraceptives, which can worsen seizure control.
key_points: Enzyme inducing antiepileptics reduce efficacy of pills and the etonogestrel implant
Copper IUD and 52 mg LNG-IUS retain efficacy because of local action
Lamotrigine levels fall with combined hormonal contraception and may need monitoring
explanation.pearls: Carbamazepine, phenytoin, topiramate over 200 mg, phenobarbital and primidone are the enzyme inducers to memorise; lamotrigine is not an inducer but its own levels drop with combined hormonal contraception.

sources.label: Family Planning Australia: Contraception with antiepileptics
